pages.text:
L-Glutamine Supplementation in COVID-19 Clinical Outcomes

what's the l-glutamine supplementation impact on morbidity and mortality of COVID patients?
Exploratory — 2026-04-10

Abstract

Background: L-glutamine is a conditionally essential amino acid that is frequently depleted during the extreme physiological stress of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection. This depletion exacerbates immune and endothelial dysfunction. Despite mechanistic rationales for exogenous supplementation, its clinical impact on coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) morbidity and mortality remains equivocal. Methods: We conducted an exploratory scoping review adhering to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-Analyses extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) guidelines. We synthesized clinical observational data, randomized controlled trials, and quasi-targeted metabolomic profiles to evaluate the efficacy of L-glutamine supplementation in COVID-19 patients. Results: Preliminary clinical data indicate that early oral L-glutamine supplementation significantly reduces hospitalization duration and intensive care unit (ICU) admission rates in non-critical patients. In critically ill ICU cohorts, intravenous supplementation added to enteral nutrition significantly decreased the need for total parenteral nutrition. However, neither intervention demonstrated a statistically significant reduction in short-term mortality. A profound metabolomic paradox emerges: while host cells require glutamine for immune homeostasis, SARS-CoV-2 exploits the same glutamine metabolic pathways for viral replication. Conclusions: L-glutamine supplementation serves as a promising adjunct therapy to reduce COVID-19 morbidity, particularly when administered early. However, its ultimate efficacy is highly dependent on baseline metabolic status and disease timing. Future large-scale, baseline-stratified trials are necessary to optimize administration protocols and mitigate potential viral replication risks.

*

1. Topic Landscape: L-Glutamine in Immune and Endothelial Function

L-glutamine is the most abundant non-essential amino acid in the human body, constituting approximately 30% of the circulating plasma free amino acid pool [15]. During periods of extreme physiological stress, catabolic states, or severe viral infections, endogenous production frequently fails to meet metabolic demands, rendering L-glutamine a "conditionally essential" nutrient [2]. L-glutamine plays a critical role in cellular homeostasis, acting as a primary oxidative fuel for rapidly dividing cells, including lymphocytes, enterocytes, and macrophages [1]. Furthermore, adequate glutamine levels are vital for preserving endothelial cellular integrity and facilitating robust, coordinated immune responses [3]. The attenuation of inflammatory cascades and the maintenance of vascular health rely heavily on optimal glutamine availability, highlighting its therapeutic potential in severe infectious diseases [1].

The pathogenesis of COVID-19, caused by SARS-CoV-2, is heavily driven by aberrant systemic inflammation and immune hyperactivation [9][12]. In severe clinical presentations, this hyperinflammatory state frequently precipitates acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), systemic tissue hypoxemia, and widespread endothelial damage [6][12], inducing profound metabolic dysregulation [13]. Advanced metabolomic and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopic profiling of COVID-19 patients consistently reveals marked alterations in the blood serum metabolome, notably characterized by significant deviations in plasma amino acid concentrations [4][7][19]. Chief among these metabolic anomalies is a severe depletion of circulating glutamine and a marked dysregulation of the
